question.text: What is the lock-in period for investments made in an Equity Linked Savings Scheme (ELSS)?

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** ELSS has the shortest lock-in period of 3 years among all tax-saving instruments eligible for deduction under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act. Other Section 80C instruments like PPF (15 years), NSC (5 years), and tax-saving FDs (5 years) have longer lock-ins. ELSS invests at least 80% in equity, combining tax benefit with equity market participation.
**The Trap:** Candidates sometimes confuse ELSS lock-in (3 years) with solution-oriented fund lock-ins. Retirement funds and children's funds have a 5-year lock-in (or till retirement/majority, whichever is earlier). The 5-year figure is a common wrong answer for ELSS.
**Cross-Exam Context:** The 3-year lock-in also means ELSS gains are treated as long-term capital gains (LTCG). After the lock-in, units can be redeemed freely. SIP investments in ELSS are locked in on a first-in-first-out basis -- each SIP installment has its own 3-year lock-in from the date of investment.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** ELSS is an excellent entry point for first-time equity investors who are already looking for tax savings. The forced 3-year lock-in acts as behavioral discipline, preventing panic selling during market downturns. Position ELSS as 'tax saving with wealth creation' rather than just a tax instrument.
